coredata.dc:title: Water-soluble organic polymer/silica composite nanofilms with improved fluorescence quantum yield
coredata.dc:creator: Feng, Baoxi
Xu, Zhen
wang, Jiayu
Gai, Ligang
coredata.dc:description: Two-dimensional photoluminescent materials have promising applications in optoelectronics, biosensors, and functional inks. However, it remains a big challenge for fabrication of water-soluble photoluminescent nanofilms with relative quantum yield higher than 50%. In this contribution, we report on facile fabrication of water-soluble organic polymer/silica composite nanofilms with relative quantum yield higher than 70%, through surface modification of organic polymer nanofilms with only a trace amount of organosilanes. The photoluminescence intensity, photostability, and quantum yield of the composite nanofilms are improved compared with individual organic polymer nanofilms. The type of organosilanes has minor effect on the enhanced optical properties of organic polymer nanofilms. The enhanced optical properties of the composite nanofilms are attributed to the strong interactions between silica and organic polymer components, based on theoretical calculations and instrumental analysis. The aqueous solution of composite nanofilms can be applied as cultivation medium for the growth of mung bean sprouts and as a fluorescent indicator for imaging the sprout tissue in vivo.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Organic polymer/silica nanofilms
Photoluminescence
High quantum yield
Organism tissue imaging

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Optical properties of samples in 50 vol% ethanol aqueous solution
Optical properties of samples in distilled water and the influence of different organosilanes
Formation of PSNFs and interactions between silica and PNFs based on MD simulations and instrumental analysis

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) UV/Vis absorbance spectra; (b) excitation spectra; (c) emission spectra with λ ex at 360 nm; (d) UV/Vis absorbance spectra and PL emission spectra; (e) emission spectra of PSNFs-4%; (f) emission spectra of PNFs; (g) plots of QY and PL intensity ratio as a function of weight percent of SiO2/PPs; (h) photostability curves; (i) time-resolved PL decay curves; the solvent for the above measurements was 50 vol% ethanol aqueous solution; the PNF concentrations for sample solutions were 0.005 mg mL−1.

(a) Photograph of PNF and PSNF aqueous solutions under daylight irradiation; (b) Photograph of PNF and PSNF aqueous solutions under irradiation at 365 nm; (c) UV/Vis absorption spectra and excitation spectra (inset); (d) PL spectra; (e) plots of QY and intensity ratio as a function of the weight percent of SiO2/PPs; (f) PL emissions with varied λ ex; (g) emission spectra of PNFs and PSNFs-4% modified with different organosilances; (h) histograms of QY (gray bars) and intensity ratio (blue bars) for PNFs and PSNFs-4% modified with different organosilanes; the numbers marked in a and b denote the weight percent of SiO2/PPs; the PNF concentrations for sample solutions were 1 mg mL−1 in a and b, and 0.005 mg mL−1 in c‒h; the solvent for a‒f is distilled water, and for g and h is ethanol aqueous solution (1:1, v/v).

MD simulation results of self-assembly of silica clusters with a segment of PNFs in water: (a) initial configuration; (b) final configuration; (c) magnified image corresponding to the squared area in b; to highlight the hydrogen bonding, the molecules of organic clusters in c are presented in a line-type model; the gray, blue, red, and yellow spheres for the segment of PNFs represent C, N, O, and S atoms, respectively; the silica clusters were saturated with H.
SEM and elemental mapping images of: (a,d) PNFs; (b,e,g) PSNFs-4%; (c,f) PSNFs-50%; the scale bars in g are 5 μm; the PSNFs were modified with TEOS in 10 vol% ethanol aqueous solution.
FT-IR spectra (a) and XPS spectra of: (b) C 1s; (c) O 1s; (d) N 1s; (e) S 2p; (f) Si 2p.

(a) Average length of sprouts as a function of the cultivation time; (b) photograph of the sprouts cultivated with distilled water and PSNFs-50% aqueous solution (0.125 mg mL−1) under excitation at 365 nm; (c‒f) LSCM images of a tissue slice of sprout cultivated with PSNF-50% medium under excitation at 405 nm; d‒f are the bright-field, dark-field, and integrated images corresponding to the squared area in c.
Formation mechanism of compound 1 through thermal condensation of CA with LCy.
XPS results of PNFs and PSNFs-4%.

head.ce:title: Water-soluble organic polymer/silica composite nanofilms with improved fluorescence quantum yield

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of Advanced Energy Materials and Chemistry, School of Chemistry and Pharmaceutical Engineering, Qilu University of Technology (Shandong Academy of Sciences), Jinan, 250353, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Institute of Advanced Energy Materials and Chemistry
School of Chemistry and Pharmaceutical Engineering
Qilu University of Technology (Shandong Academy of Sciences)

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Two-dimensional photoluminescent materials have promising applications in optoelectronics, biosensors, and functional inks. However, it remains a big challenge for fabrication of water-soluble photoluminescent nanofilms with relative quantum yield higher than 50%. In this contribution, we report on facile fabrication of water-soluble organic polymer/silica composite nanofilms with relative quantum yield higher than 70%, through surface modification of organic polymer nanofilms with only a trace amount of organosilanes. The photoluminescence intensity, photostability, and quantum yield of the composite nanofilms are improved compared with individual organic polymer nanofilms. The type of organosilanes has minor effect on the enhanced optical properties of organic polymer nanofilms. The enhanced optical properties of the composite nanofilms are attributed to the strong interactions between silica and organic polymer components, based on theoretical calculations and instrumental analysis. The aqueous solution of composite nanofilms can be applied as cultivation medium for the growth of mung bean sprouts and as a fluorescent indicator for imaging the sprout tissue in vivo.

Polymer nanofilms are formed through a self-assembly formation mechanism when polymer powders are dissolved into water with the assistance of ultrasound. After addition of organosilanes into water, in situ hydrolysis of organosilane molecules occurs on the surface of film surface, resulting in formation of organic polymer/silica composite nanofilms with improved fluorescence quantum yield and photostability.
Image 1

ce:section.ce:para: Photoluminescent materials have attracted considerable interest due to their wide applications in sensors [1–7], photocatalysis [8,9], cellular imaging [10], cancer diagnosis and therapy [11–13], energy-storage systems [14], and organic light-emitting diodes [15,16]. For real applications, high fluorescence intensity and quantum yield (QY) are highly desirous for luminophors with concentration as low as possible. However, most of luminophors in previous reports have relatively low QY values which were usually lower than 50% [9–12]. To improve the fluorescence QY, two main strategies have been designed including doping with foreign elements [3,17,18] and surface passivation with reducing agents [19,20] or organic/inorganic modifiers [21–28]. For example, hydrothermal treatment of citric acid with ethylene diamine offered N-doped carbon dots (CDs) with a high QY value of 94% [18]. As documented in literature, doping CDs with foreign elements can introduce a new type of surface states in which trapped electrons enable a high yield of radiative recombination [3].

Surface passivation through reduction of carboxyl groups with NaBH4 contributes positively to the enhanced QY of CDs [19,20]. With polyethylene glycol (PEG) and PEG-derivatives as passivating agents, CDs with enhanced QY can be achieved by removing the surface states that act as nonradiative recombination centers [21] or stabilizing the surface states that facilitate radiative recombination [22]. Organosilanes have also functioned as surface modifiers to improve the QY of SiCDs [26,27] and N-doped CDs [28]. However, it remains a big challenge to reveal the passivation mechanism with respect to the organosilane-assisted enhancement in QY.

Two-dimensional (2D) photoluminescent materials have promising applications in optoelectronics, biosensors, and functional inks [24,29–32]. However, reports on organic polymer-based photoluminescent 2D materials are rare in literature [15,16]. Recently, we have developed a simple method for producing organic polymer nanofilms (PNFs) by direct dissolution of the corresponding polymer powders into water with the assistance of ultrasound [33]. The as-obtained PNFs exhibit relatively high QY (>65%). Of particular interest is that the QY of PNFs can be dramatically enhanced by surface modification with only a trace amount of organosilanes. Also, there has been no report on photoluminescent organic polymer/silica composite nanofilms (PSNFs). In this contribution, we report on the fabrication of 2D fluorescent PSNFs with enhanced photoluminescence (PL) and QY through a simple sol–gel method.

In a typical process, citric acid (CA) and l-cysteine (LCy) with CA/LCy molar ratio of 2:1 were thoroughly ground and transferred into a beaker. The beaker was placed into an electric oven and kept at the temperature of 160 °C for 1 h, yielding yellow brown polymer powders (PPs). For preparation of PSNFs, 65 mg of PPs were dissolved into 65 mL of ethanol/aqueous solution (0–50 vol% for ethanol). The mixture was treated with ultrasound at room temperature for 5 min, resulting in formation of polymer nanofilms (PNFs). Then, 10 μL of tetraethoxysilane (TEOS) was added by using a digital microsyringe. The mixture was stirred at room temperature for 12 h, producing PSNFs-4% colloid solution, where 4% represents the theoretical weight percent of SiO2 to PPs. In control experiment, SiO2 colloids were prepared with individual TEOS. Also, different silane coupling agents were used and the theoretical weight percent of SiO2 to PPs was varied in the range of 0.5–50 wt%.

Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) and elemental mapping measurements were performed on a Hitachi Regulus 8220 field-emission scanning electron microscope. Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were recorded on a Shimadzu IRPrestige-21 infrared spectrometer using pressed KBr discs. Measurements by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) were conducted on a Thermo Fisher Scientific ESCALAB 250 X-ray photoelectron spectrometer, using monochromatic Al Kα radiation (1486.6 eV). The high-resolution spectra were calibrated by setting C 1s at 284.8 eV. The fitting of high-resolution XPS spectra was performed through Gaussian and Lorentz functions. Laser scanning confocal microscopy (LSCM) images were taken on a Leica SP8 laser scanning confocal microscope. Time-of-flight liquid chromatography-mass spectrometry (TOF LC/MS) measurements were conducted on an Agilent 6520 quadrupole TOF LC/MS spectrometer. Room-temperature photoluminescence (PL) spectra were recorded on a Hitachi F-4600FL fluorescence spectrometer. Ultraviolet/visible (UV/Vis) absorption spectra were collected by a TU-1901 UV/Vis spectrophotometer (Beijing, China). Quantum yield (QY) of samples was estimated according to eq (1) expressed as [34,35].
(1)
Q Y = Q
Y
r
×
I
I
r
×
A
r
A
×
n
2
n
r
2
where r refers to the reference, and I, A, and n represent the emission intensity, absorbance intensity, and refractive index of solvent, respectively. In the present case, quinine sulfate in 0.1 M H2SO4 aqueous solution was used as the reference. To avoid the inner filter effect, the concentration of sample aqueous solutions was set at 0.005 mg mL−1.

Molecular dynamics (MD) simulations were carried out through the Materials Studio (Accelrys Inc) software after assignment of charges and potentials to each atom. The simple point charge model was employed for water molecules to accurately describe a water-solution environment [36]. The parameters for each like-site interaction were provided by the COMPASS force field, and the long-range electrostatic interactions were calculated through the Ewald method [37]. The Smart Minimizer method was employed to minimize the energy of the initial configuration. Then, the simulations were equilibrated at constant temperature (298.15 K) and volume (NVT) for 10 ns Atomic coordinates were saved every 200 ps

Compound 1 (Scheme 1 ) was used as a probe to simulate the self-assembly formation of PNFs in water by construction of a 3D periodic lattice (x = 43.5 Å, y = 43.5 Å, and z = 43.5 Å), which contains 32 molecules of compound 1 and 2080 water molecules. The final configuration at 10 ns was taken as the segment of PNFs. Then, two silica clusters were placed in the blank region of the lattice. Every silica cluster contains 15 O and 8 Si atoms saturated with 14 H atoms. Another 10 ns was allowed to run to achieve the final configuration of the segment of PSNFs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optical properties of samples in 50 vol% ethanol aqueous solution
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 1 shows the optical properties of the samples. Different from that of individual SiO2, the UV/Vis absorbance spectra of PNFs and PSNFs exhibit a shoulder peak around 245 nm and a broad peak centered around 360 nm (Fig. 1a), corresponding to π→π* and n→π* electron transitions [2,38], respectively. Compared with the former transition, the latter transition related to trapping of excited-state energy by surface states contributes more to fluorescence emission [3,4]. Note that there are slight red shift in peak position and slight increase in peak intensity of the latter transition for PSNFs compared with PNFs, implying the enhanced PL of PSNFs.

In concert with the two types of electron transitions in the UV/Vis spectra, the excitation spectra of PNFs and PSNFs present strong peaks centered at 252 and 373 nm (Fig. 1b). The occurrence of shoulder peaks centered at 232 nm in the excitation spectra of PSNFs is attributed to the silica component. With excitation wavelength (λ ex) at 360 nm, the PL intensity of PNSFs is higher than that of PNFs (Fig. 1c). The PL intensity of PNSFs increases with increasing silica concentration from 0.5% to 4%, while decreases as the silica concentration increases over 4%. This result suggests silica, even in a small amount, can stabilize the surface states that facilitate radiative recombination [22]. It should be pointed out that the mixture of PNFs with silica colloids, referred to as PNFs/SiO2-4% in which silica accounts for 4 wt% of PNFs, exhibits similar absorbance but enhanced PL compared with individual PNFs (Fig. 1d). This result is attributed to the facile interactions between silica and PNFs through hydrogen bonding (discussion below).

Under excitations from 240 to 400 nm, PSNFs-4% exhibits a λ ex-independent emission behavior with nearly constant maximum emissions centered at 452 nm (Fig. 1e), similar to the situation for PNFs with maximum emissions centered at 448 ± 4 nm (Fig. 1f). The λ ex-independent emission behavior is interpreted that the wealthy organic fluorophores in PNFs, such as pyridine derivatives derived from thermal condensation of CA with LCy [38,39], remain their optical characteristics in PSNFs. The optimal emission peak of PSNFs-4% is achieved with λ ex at 360 nm. The full width at half-maximum of the optimal emission is 80 nm, nearly equal to that of 81 nm for PNFs. This result indicates uniform band structures of surface states [5].

Apart from the increase in PL intensity, the QY of PSNFs is dramatically enhanced in comparison with that of PNFs (Fig. 1g). For example, PSNFs-4% exhibits a high QY of 87%, which is higher by 21% than that for PNFs and much higher than that (˂ 50%) for most carbon-based luminophors [5,6,9–12]. Even for PSNFs-0.5%, the QY value can be enhanced by 17% compared with individual PNFs. Another difference is that the photostability of PSNFs is enhanced compared with PNFs. Taking PSNFs-4% as an example, the retained PL intensity after irradiation at 360 nm for 1 h is 98.1%, higher by 1.4% than that for PNFs (Fig. 1h).

Fig. 1i shows the time-resolved emission spectra of the samples in ethanol‒water (1:1, v/v) solution. The luminescence time profiles match well with exponential fitting functions, yielding lifetimes of 11.88 and 11.54 ns for PNFs and PSNFs, respectively. The lifetimes for PNFs and PSNFs are close to those of DNA-derived biodots (10.44 ns) [40] and CDs derived from thermal condensation of CA with LCy (10.77 ns, 12.11 ns) [3,39], but higher than those of O-doped CDs (7.45 ns) [3], O,N-codoped CDs/polymer dots (1.87–8.8 ns) [10,20,41], and graphene quantum dots (7.52 ns) [13]. The relatively long lifetimes for PNFs and PSNFs are attributed to the intrinsic and trap-mediated luminescence processes [41]. The nearly equivalent lifetimes for PNFs and PSNFs indicate the organic fluorophores in PNFs have not been altered by silica modification.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optical properties of samples in distilled water and the influence of different organosilanes
ce:section.ce:para: Under daylight, the PSNF aqueous solutions are highly transparent with PSNF concentration at 1 mg mL−1 (Fig. 2 a), indicating a water solubility of PSNFs. Meanwhile, fluorescence can be discerned from PNF and PSNF aqueous solutions under daylight. Also, both PNF and PSNF solutions emit strong blue luminescence under irradiation at 365 nm, especially for PSNFs with SiO2/PNFs weight ratio ranging from 2% to 16% (Fig. 2b). These results reflect excellent PL characteristics of PSNFs in water.

Compared with those for PSNFs in ethanol aqueous solution (1:1, v/v) (Fig. 1a and b), the peaks centered at 346 nm in the UV/Vis and at 360 nm in the excitation spectra (Fig. 2c and inset), corresponding to n→π* electron transitions, blue shift for PSNFs in water. Also, the position of the maximum emission peak shifts by 15 nm to the left for PSNFs in water (Fig. 2d), compared with that for PSNFs in ethanol aqueous solution (Fig. 1c). These results are attributed to the solvent effect. Similar to the situation for PSNFs in ethanol aqueous solution, the PL intensity and QY for PSNFs in water are increased compared with those for PNFs (Fig. 2e). From the QY point of view, PSNFs-2% exhibits the best performance (Fig. 2e). The QY of PSNFs-2% is 80.7%, higher by 15.1% than that of PNFs. Under excitations with λ ex ranging from 240 to 400 nm, the maximum PL emissions of PSNFs-2% in water are centered at 437 ± 4 nm (Fig. 2f). This result indicates PSNFs in water remain the λ ex-independent emission behavior.

To explore whether the types of organosilanes influence the PL emission of PNFs, several other organosilanes were tested as modifiers including dimethyldimethoxysilane (DMDMOS), γ-(2,3-epoxypropoxy)propyltrimethoxysilane (KH-560), octyltriethoxysilane (OTEOS), heptadecafluorodecyl-1H,1H,2H, 2H-triethoxysilane (17F), and dodecyltriethoxysilane (DTEOS). It is found that (i) introducing a small amount of the aforementioned organosilanes can improve the PL intensity and QY of PNFs (Fig. 2g and h); and (ii) the length of side carbon chains and functional groups (epoxy and halogen) of organosilanes have minor effects on the optical properties of PNFs, in the case that equal molar organosilanes were employed.

On the basis of the above analysis, we conclude that (1) modification of PNFs with only a trace amount of in situ formed silica not only dramatically increases the PL intensity and QY, but also facilitates to improve the photostability; and (2) there is a minor discrepancy between the different organosilanes in improving the optical properties of PNFs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Formation of PSNFs and interactions between silica and PNFs based on MD simulations and instrumental analysis
ce:section.ce:para: When PPs are dissolved into water with the assistance of ultrasound, PNFs are formed through a self-assembly formation mechanism (Scheme 2 a). After addition of a small amount of organosilanes, the molecules of organosilanes tend to be anchored by PNFs through van der Waals forces due to the hydrophobicity of organosilane molecules (Scheme 2b). Given the pH of PNF solution is ca. 4, in situ hydrolysis of organosilane molecules occurs on PNF surface, resulting in formation of PSNFs (Scheme 2c).

To deep understand the passivation mechanism for the improved QY of PSNFs, MD simulations were performed by construction of a 3D periodic lattice (x = 43.5 Å, y = 43.5 Å, and z = 43.5 Å), into which silica clusters and a segment of PNFs were introduced (Fig. 3 a). The segment of PNFs in the form of organic molecular clusters results from the final configuration of self-assembled compound 1 (Scheme 1), which was separated from PNFs through the column chromatography technique and determined through the TOF/LC-MS technique (Supplementary material, S1 and S2). After simulations, we found that the silica clusters tend to approach to each other and interweave with the segment of PNFs through hydrogen bonding (Fig. 3b and c). The formation of hydrogen bonding is in the forms of SiOH⋯O/N and SiO⋯HO/N with bond length between adjacent donor and acceptor O/N being smaller than 3 Å [42–44].

The MD simulations depict a scenario that silica clusters interweave with the segment of PNFs. To confirm this result, the morphologies of PNFs and PSNFs modified with TEOS were examined through the SEM technique. As shown in Fig. 4 a,d, PNFs exhibit a smooth surface, and the thickness of PNFs is in the range of 20–40 nm (Fig. 4a, inset). After modification with weight percent of SiO2 to PPs ranging from 4% to 50%, PSNFs retain the wrinkled film structure (Fig. 4b,c,e,f). Although some silica nanoparticles occur on the film surface, elemental mapping images of PSNFs-4% reveal uniform areal distributions of C, N, O, S, and Si (Fig. 4g). The SEM and elemental mapping results suggest that the silica clusters, resulting from hydrolysis of organosilane molecules captured by PNFs, may be interweaved with the organic molecular clusters of PNFs. Further increase in the amount of organosilane relative to PPs, such as for PSNFs-50%, produces many silica particles attached on film surface due to hydrolysis of free organosilane molecules in solution.

The interweaving of silica clusters with organic molecular clusters of PNFs ensures strong interactions between the two components. Fig. 5 a shows the FT-IR spectra of the samples. The peaks marked in Fig. 5a correspond separately to v(CO) stretching vibration (1719 cm−1), v(CC)/δ(OH) vibrations (1636 cm−1), v(CN)/δ(NH) vibrations (1517 cm−1), γ(OH) (1398 cm−1) and ρ(OH) (775 cm−1) deforming vibrations, and v(CN,O,S) stretching vibrations (1270‒1130 cm−1) [2,4,45]. The peaks centered at 1079, 956, and 804 cm−1 in SiO2 spectrum correspond to v(SiO) vibrations [46]. It is difficult to discern the peaks characteristic of silica component in PSNFs-4% spectrum. This result is consistent with the interweaving of silica clusters with organic molecular clusters as aforementioned. Compared with the PNFs and PSNFs-4% spectra, PSNFs-50% spectrum exhibits weakened peaks belonging to the PNF component and strong peaks characteristic of the silica component. A noteworthy point is that the peaks in PSNFs-50% spectrum corresponding to v(CN,O,S) and v(SiO) red shift in comparison with those in PNFs and SiO2 spectra, respectively. This result is indicative of strong interactions between silica and PNF components in PSNFs.

The survey XPS spectrum of PSNFs-4% evidences the existence of elemental C, O, N, S, and Si (data not shown). Through Gaussian and Lorentz functions, the components of C, O, N, S, and Si can be resolved from the high-resolution spectra of C 1s, O 1s, N 1s, S 2p, and Si 2P (Fig. 5b‒f and Table 1 ). It is found that the CO/N and HOCO components of C 1s and the components of O 1s, N 1s, and S 2p for PSNFs-4% are shifted to higher binding energies compared with those for PNFs. This result confirms strong interactions between silica and PNF components in PSNFs, in concert with the FT-IR result.

The Si 2p spectrum of PSNFs-4% can be resolved into two peaks centered at 103.5 and 102.7 eV. The former peak corresponds to SiOSi, and the latter peak is attributed to SiO binding with nonsilica substrate (referred to as SiO*) such as carbon nanotubes [47]. Also, the concentrations of COOH and NH components are decreased for PSNFs-4% in comparison with those for PNFs. These results consolidate the silica component strongly binds with the PNF component through hydrogen bonding as depicted in Fig. 3c. Thus, we infer that the hydrogen bonding between the silica and the surface groups of PNFs may stabilize the surface states that facilitate radiative recombination [22].

To confirm the biocompatibility of PSNFs, PSNFs-50% (0.125 mg mL−1) aqueous solution was taken as a medium to cultivate the mung bean sprouts. In control experiments, the mung bean sprouts were cultivated with distilled water. It is found that the growth of sprouts is not affected by using PSNFs-50% (0.125 mg mL−1) aqueous solution as the cultivation medium (Fig. 6 a and Fig. S3), indicating a high biocompatibility of PSNFs-50% in vivo. Under excitation at 365 nm, the sprout cultivated in PSNFs-50% medium emits strong blue PL whereas the PL emission from the sprout cultivated in distilled water cannot be discerned (Fig. 6b). Under excitation at 405 nm, the tissue structure of the sprout cultivated with PSNF medium can be clearly discerned by the LSCM technique (Fig. 6c‒f). These results indicate PSNFs can be used as a fluorescence indicator for imaging sprout tissues in vivo, although the phagocytosis mechanism of the sprout towards PSNFs has not been well understood.

In summary, PSNFs have been prepared through a sol‒gel method involving surface modification of PNFs with a trace amount of organosilances. The as-formed PSNFs exhibit improved PL intensity, photostability, and QY compared with individual PNFs. Surface modification with 4 wt% silica relative to PPs has nearly no influence on the fluorescence lifetime of PNFs. Computational calculations and instrumental analysis confirm strong interactions between the silica and PNF components through hydrogen bonding. PSNFs exhibit a high biocompatibility for cultivation of mung bean sprouts with strong PL emission. The PSNFs-based fluorescence indicator holds a great potential for imaging organism tissue in vivo.
The authors declare no conflicts of interest.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China (51272143) and Natural Science Foundation of Shandong Province (ZR2017QB003).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is the Supplementary data to this article:

Supplementary material contains the process of column chromatography, TOF LC/MS analysis, and photographs of mung bean sprouts.
Multimedia component 1 Multimedia component 1

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jlumin.2019.03.066.
